schools.summary: Ellicott distinguishes Sheba from Seba, places the kingdom in Arabia Felix, and reads the 'hard questions' as the deep enigmatic sayings of Eastern philosophy, not puerile riddles.
Gill gathers the traditions of the queen (Maqueda, Balkis) and identifies Sheba as the metropolis of Arabia Felix.
Matthew Henry: she came not for trade but from curiosity and, chiefly, to know 'the name of the LORD' and be instructed in the things of God.
Clarke: the 'queen of the south' (Matt. 12:42), the name Saba meaning 'the south'; the hard questions are riddles, the Septuagint's enigmas.

sides.position: Deep enigmatic and metaphysical sayings, the great moral problems of Job, Proverbs, and Ecclesiastes
Riddles, parables, and enigmas in which Eastern wisdom delighted

schools.summary: Keil & Delitzsch read 'train' as riches not an army and the things 'in her heart' as riddles, not religious sayings; Ellicott details the famed spices, gold, and gems of Arabia.
Clarke heaps up classical testimony (Virgil, Pliny) to the Sabaeans' wealth, spices, and their rule by queens.
Gill pictures her retinue of courtiers, principal men, and servants.

debates.question: Were the matters 'in her heart' religious enquiries or enigmatic riddles?

sides.position: Dark sayings and riddles laid before Solomon's wisdom

sides.holders: the earlier commentators (per Keil and Delitzsch)

schools.summary: Gill: Solomon answered every question, expounded her riddles, solved her objections, and satisfied her in all things.
Clarke: riddles, problems, and apologues were the principal part of Eastern wisdom (compare Samson's riddle).
Keil & Delitzsch press the emphatic 'nothing hid' and let the narrative move from what she hears to what she sees.
Matthew Henry: whether natural, moral, political, or divine, Solomon answered all, so that a divine sentence was in the lips of the king.
Gill: she perceived his wisdom by his answers on all sorts of science, natural, civil, and divine.
Clarke: she saw his wisdom in his answers to her subtle questions; the house is probably his own palace.
Keil & Delitzsch: the house she saw was Solomon's palace, not the temple.
Keil & Delitzsch unfold the officers, ministers, seats, apparel, and drink of the ordered court, holding the received 'ascent' to the temple.
Gill notes the daily provision and various dishes of the royal table.
Clarke reads the last clause as 'the burnt-offerings which he offered' (with the ancient versions), and 'no more spirit in her' as a real swoon.
Matthew Henry keeps both senses, but crowns the list with Solomon's devotion in going up to the house of the LORD.

debates.question: Does the last clause mean the ascent to the temple or the burnt offerings offered there?

sides.position: The burnt offerings Solomon offered in the house of the LORD (Vulgate, LXX, Chaldee, Syriac, Arabic, Luther)
The ascent or stairway by which the king went up to worship

schools.summary: Gill: she spoke when 'a little come to herself,' the astonishment giving way to speech.
Keil & Delitzsch: she says with astonishment that she had not heard the half, the once-incredible report now proved true.
Matthew Henry: far from repenting her journey, she owns it was worth the coming, for here the truth exceeded both fame and fancy.
Gill: she had not disbelieved all, but thought the reports too much magnified; the reality rebukes her caution.
Keil & Delitzsch: her wonder overflows from the king to his servants and up to Jehovah his God.
Matthew Henry: those brought to commune with God, and glorified saints much more, will say the half was not told them (1 Cor. 2:9).
Gill: happy the men of Israel with so wise and good a king, and the attendants who hear his wisdom.
Clarke: natural expressions from one in her state of mind.
Matthew Henry: she was tempted to envy the servants; blessed are those who dwell in Christ's house (Ps. 84:4).
Gill: she now had better notions of the God of Israel than when she left her own land.
Keil & Delitzsch: she praises Jehovah for his eternal love to Israel; the older theologians inferred (with Matt. 12:42) her conversion.
Matthew Henry: she blesses God as giver of Solomon's wisdom and throne, made king to do judgment and justice, not for pomp.

themes: blessing the Giver not the gift

schools.summary: Gill: the 120 talents equal Hiram's gift and fulfil the prophecy of the gold of Sheba (Ps. 72:15).
Clarke reckons the gold's great value and notes that verse 13 seems misplaced, belonging after this verse.
Matthew Henry: the gift fulfils prophecy and prefigures the gold and spices the magi brought to Christ (Matt. 2:11).

outliers.note: Observes that verse 13, which closes the account of the queen, is misplaced and should be read straight after verse 10.

themes: a type of homage to Christ

schools.summary: Gill: much of Solomon's gold came not from Sheba but from Hiram's fleet out of Ophir, which he places in India.
Keil & Delitzsch: the almug wood was made into supports or a balustraded staircase for temple and palace, and into harps for the singers.
Clarke canvasses the tree's identity (thyine, lignum vitae, pine, cypress, coloured/rose wood), best of all for inlaying.
Gill: the 'pillars' are rather terraces or a causeway with rails, the very ascent the queen admired.
Keil & Delitzsch: props or a balustraded stair, and harps and psalteries for the temple singers; such wood was never seen again.

debates.question: What did Solomon make of the almug wood?

sides.position: Props and a balustraded staircase for temple and palace
Terraces, a causeway, or rails for the ascent from palace to temple

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil & Delitzsch: the terms mark the queen's inferiority (her gift a tribute) and Solomon's royal bounty answering to his greatness.
Gill: Solomon gave her all her desire.
Clarke reports the Abyssinian legend that she desired and bore a son, Menilek, who introduced the Jewish religion to Saba.
Matthew Henry: those who apply to Christ find him greater, wiser, and kinder than Solomon, giving more than we can ask (Eph. 3:20).

outliers.note: Records the Abyssinian tradition that 'all her desire' meant progeny, and that her son Menilek succeeded her and brought in the Jewish religion.

schools.summary: Keil & Delitzsch: 666 is a round, approximate figure covering all Solomon's revenue, not merely taxes; the Ophir fleet came only every three years.
Gill reckons the sum near three millions of pounds.
Clarke reckons it higher still, and notes it stood independent of the Arabian kings' tribute and the merchants' duties.
Matthew Henry calls 666 an ominous number (Rev. 13:18), suspects the apes and peacocks a sign of wantonness, and recalls that Solomon asked wisdom, not wealth, and wrote of its vanity.

sides.position: A round, approximate figure of the total yearly revenue
An ominous number, recalling the number of the beast

schools.summary: Gill: the merchants' payments were customs on imported goods.
Keil & Delitzsch set the verse within the sum of Solomon's revenue; Ellicott notes 'governors' (pechah) is a foreign, perhaps later, word for tributary officers.
Gill: the targets were larger, body-covering shields of beaten gold, as the Carthaginians made.
Keil & Delitzsch reckon about seventeen and a half pounds of gold to each target.
Clarke likens the tsinnah to a Highland targe with a projecting dagger.
Matthew Henry: made for state not service, shields signifying that royal power is for the defence of the good.
Ellicott fixes the 'pound' as the maneh of a hundred shekels; Keil & Delitzsch count five hundred pieces in all, laid up in the cedar armoury.
Gill marks these as the lesser sort of shield.
Clarke computes the great value of targets and shields.
Matthew Henry: like the targets, these were for pomp, declaring the king's strength spent on defence.

themes: the house of the forest of Lebanon

schools.summary: Ellicott traces the history of ivory (via Tyre from India and Africa) and reads its emblems (lion of Judah, twelve tribes, exalted seat); Keil & Delitzsch note the throne was ornamented, not made, of ivory.
Gill: the white ivory denotes the purity and equity of his judgment, with an allusion to the great white throne (Rev. 20:11).
Matthew Henry: a stately throne for giving laws, receiving ambassadors, and rendering judgment.
Gill: the six steps raised the seat so all might see the king and hear his sentence.
Clarke reads a round-topped, two-armed chair; the 'stays' are literally yadoth, 'hands.'
Keil & Delitzsch read a seat arched or rounded behind, flanked by carved lions at the arm-rests.
Matthew Henry: the golden lions were a memorandum of the courage to execute judgment without fearing the face of man (Prov. 28:1).
Gill: a lion on each side of every step, a symbol of royal power, as the Egyptians set lions under the throne of Orus.
Keil & Delitzsch register the historian's boast that no like throne was made in any kingdom.
Matthew Henry: the golden lions set before the king at every ascent the courage that judgment demands.

themes: the tribes and the king's courage

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil & Delitzsch: silver came in floods by the triennial Tarshish fleet and so was counted as nothing (2 Chron. 9:27).
Gill: the gold vessels came of the abundance from Ophir, tribute, and presents.
Matthew Henry: Solomon enjoyed his wealth without grudging, yet the despised silver preaches that plenty cheapens what it multiplies, and spiritual riches should lessen our esteem of earthly ones.

Ellicott debates Tarshish as Tartessus in Spain versus an eastern (Indian) voyage, resolving it by a circumnavigation of Africa; Clarke takes the 'apes' for monkeys; Keil & Delitzsch open the closing summary.
Gill takes 'Tharshish' for the sea itself, the Tarsic (Indian) sea washing Ophir.
Matthew Henry: the apes and peacocks hint at a court grown childish and wanton with prosperity.

debates.question: Where or what is the 'navy of Tharshish'?

sides.position: Tartessus in Spain, or 'ships of Tarshish' as a class of large ship, with the eastern cargo explained by a circumnavigation of Africa
The Tarsic or Indian sea, near Ophir, whither both fleets were bound

schools.summary: Keil & Delitzsch: Solomon 'became greater,' the verb of an ascent that could be lost; all the world sought him.
Clarke reduces the greatness to figures, reckoning the king's daily income.
Gill: in this surpassing greatness Solomon was an eminent type of Christ.
Matthew Henry: this was he who asked wisdom before wealth, and who wrote a book to show the vanity of all worldly things.
Gill: 'all the earth' means its principal inhabitants; the wisdom was God's peculiar gift, put in Solomon's heart.
Keil & Delitzsch: those drawn by his wisdom brought presents year by year, the seeking and the revenue one movement.
Matthew Henry: they came to be instructed in the things of God, a rebuke to those who will not seek God by a greater than Solomon (Matt. 12:42).
Gill: presents brought to introduce the givers into the king's presence.
Clarke reads them as tribute and details the kinds: bullion, rich garments, armour, spices, horses, and mules.
Keil & Delitzsch: annual gifts from foreigners of all lands, answering to Solomon's greatness.
Matthew Henry: presents sent yearly by neighbouring princes to keep his friendship, from admiration of his wisdom.
Gill: the chariots and horsemen were prudent provision against sudden attack in a time of peace.
Clarke refers the numbers to the earlier notice of Solomon's stalls and horsemen (1 Kings 4:26).
Ellicott reads chariots as the sign of empire and one of the powers holding the kingdom together, the chariot cities being fortified posts; Keil & Delitzsch note the verse repeats an earlier record.
Gill: an hyperbolical expression of the vast silver from Tarshish.
Clarke: plenty destroyed silver's value, and cedar grew as common as the sycamore.
Keil & Delitzsch call it hyperbole for enormous quantities; Ellicott warns the wealth enriched only the capital and court, sowing the division that broke out under Rehoboam.
Matthew Henry: plenty of worldly wealth makes it the less valuable; spiritual riches should make earthly ones cheaper still.
Gill: bringing horses from Egypt seems contrary to the command of the Law.
Clarke: God prohibited the importing of horses (Deut. 17:16), but Solomon little regarded it; the word 'mikveh' is obscure or corrupt, and he canvasses many solutions.
Ellicott: 'linen yarn' is likely an error; the sense is a troop of merchants obtaining a troop of horses at a fixed price, with the LXX variant 'from Tekoa.'

debates.question: What is the obscure word rendered 'linen yarn' (mikveh)?

sides.position: An error for 'a troop/company' obtaining horses at a fixed price
Tribute farmed by the king's merchants at a price

sides.holders: the LXX and Vulgate (per Clarke and Ellicott)

schools.summary: Gill reckons the six hundred shekels at about seventy-five pounds.
Keil & Delitzsch take 'chariot' for the vehicle with its team; Ellicott notes the regular commerce and the Hittite and Syrian buyers, confirmed as a real power by Egyptian and Assyrian inscriptions.
Clarke: the ordinary prices, the Hittites a remnant settled in Syria, and the monopoly by which all came through Solomon's servants.
